turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 23-997, Stanley versus City of Sanford.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: The Americans with Disabilities Act bars employers from discriminating against qualified individuals on the basis of disability. The statute defines a qualified individual as someone who, with or without reasonable accommodation, can perform the essential functions of the employment position that she holds or desires. This case asks us to decide whether that definition extends to retirees, like petitioner Karen Stanley, who neither hold nor desire a job when they experience an alleged act of discrimination. To answer that question, we turn as we must to the instructions Congress has given us. When the ADA defines the term qualified individual, it uses present tense verbs. The statute makes it unlawful to discriminate against someone who can perform the essential functions of the job she holds or desires. Those verbs indicate that the statute protects individuals who, with or without reasonable accommodation, are able to do the job they hold or seek when they suffer discrimination. By the same token, those verbs suggest the statute does not reach retirees who neither hold nor desire a job when an act of discrimination occurs. Other parts of the statutes reinforce our assessment. So, for example, recall that the ADA prohibits discrimination against an individual who can perform essential job functions with or without a reasonable accommodation. And a reasonable accommodation, the statute says, refers to things like altering training materials, accommodations that make perfect sense for current or prospective employees, but not retired ones. Even beyond that, a number of other statutory clues discussed in our opinion, and I will not bore you with, also indicate that to prevail in a suit for employment discrimination under the ADA, a plaintiff must plead and prove that she either held or desired a job and could perform its functions with or without a reasonable accommodation at the time of the employer's alleged act of discrimination. Now saying that much is enough to answer the question we took this case to resolve and the circuit split that led to it. But for the first time in her merits briefing, Ms. Stanley asked us to address still another question. Although her petition for certiorari focused on discrimination against retirees, she now tells us that she also suffered discrimination while she was employed. So even if the ADA protects only those who hold or seek a job when they suffer discrimination, she says, we should decide whether her case satisfies that standard. Ordinarily, this Court rejects efforts like that to inject new questions into a case after we have granted review. But a plurality of the Court finds it profitable to make an exception here to outline three separate theories retirees like Ms. Stanley can still pursue consistent with the ADA's terms. Those theories, we conclude, cannot help Ms. Stanley herself because of case-specific considerations, including deficiencies in her complaint and her failure to pursue the theories below. But as we explain, future plaintiffs might avoid those missteps, and even Ms. Stanley herself might try to cure them in future proceedings. For these reasons and others detailed in our opinion, the judgment of the Eleventh Circuit is affirmed. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment which Justice Barrett has joined. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion which Justice Sotomayor has joined in part.
